name: Sutherland's Sword of Damocles
short_description: The first head-mounted 3D display with real-time head tracking, displaying computer-generated stereoscopic wireframe graphics — the founding prototype of every spatial headset that followed.
long_description: Built by Ivan Sutherland and his students Bob Sproull, Quintin Foster, and Danny Cohen, the Sword of Damocles established the core HMD paradigm in 1968: a head-worn optical see-through display linked to a computer that updated a stereoscopic wireframe image in real time based on the wearer's head position. The headset was heavy enough to require suspension from a ceiling-mounted mechanical arm — the source of its name — and the computer generated only simple geometric shapes such as a suspended cube. Yet the system defined the two structural commitments that every spatial headset since has honoured: continuous positional sensing and perspective-correct rendering for each eye. Early experiments began at MIT Lincoln Laboratory from 1966; the integrated system was completed at the University of Utah. Sutherland described the concept's horizon in his earlier 1965 essay 'The Ultimate Display', and the 1968 device was its first physical realisation.
ai_note: No AI or ML — the system used real-time matrix multiplication and vector graphics driven by a general-purpose computer; head tracking was mechanical/electrical, not learned.

images.caption: Ivan Sutherland wearing the head-mounted display suspended from a ceiling-mounted mechanical arm, University of Utah, circa 1968.
images.alt_text: A person wearing a large, bulky head-mounted display that is connected via mechanical linkages to an arm attached to the ceiling, in a university lab setting.

sources.title: The Sword of Damocles (virtual reality)
sources.access_notes: Wikipedia article summarising the system, its six subsystems, institution timeline (MIT Lincoln Lab 1966 → University of Utah, 1968), and collaborators (Sproull, Foster, Cohen).

sources.title: A Head-Mounted Three Dimensional Display
sources.access_notes: Sutherland's primary technical publication describing the system. Presented at the 1968 Fall Joint Computer Conference (AFIPS). ACM Digital Library reprint.

sources.title: The Ultimate Display
sources.access_notes: Sutherland's earlier essay articulating the conceptual goal that the 1968 device physically realised: a display room in which the computer controls the existence of matter.

sources.title: History of Virtual Reality
sources.access_notes: Corroborates 1968 date, University of Utah, ceiling-mounted arm, wireframe graphics, and Sproull as key collaborator.
